title: Consolidate fallback value storage routing to prevent source-mode interaction bugs
author: salvatore-campagna
body:
### Description

## Problem

PR #153462 adds `on_failure=ignore` for columnar mode. The question "a value could not be indexed
normally, where should it go?" is answered in at least four places independently:
`DocumentParser.parseObjectOrField` pre-captures to `_ignored_source` based on
`syntheticSourceMode()` and `sourceKeepMode`; `DocumentParserContext.enforceSingleValue` writes to
the `._on_failure` failure column based on source mode; `IgnoreMalformedStoredValues` call sites
write to `._ignore_malformed` or stored fields based on source mode and index version. None of
these were designed to own routing decisions, yet all of them do. This makes the routing logic
impossible to test in one place: covering all combinations of source mode, failure reason, and call
site requires tests scattered across the codebase, and the interactions between call sites fall
through the gaps entirely.

The known instance is `parseObjectOrField` pre-capturing to `_ignored_source` before calling
`fieldMapper.parse()`, while `enforceSingleValue` inside `fieldMapper.parse()` also writes the
violated value to the `._on_failure` failure column. For fields with fallback synthetic source mode
or `sourceKeepMode == ARRAYS`, both paths fire for the same violation. This will not be the last
such interaction as the feature grows.

## Goal

The storage routing responsibility needs a single owner so that adding a new mechanism cannot
silently interact with an existing one. The `parseObjectOrField` pre-capture pattern is the most
concrete place to address, as it makes a storage decision independently of the field mapper. Any
solution should guarantee that a value is written to exactly one fallback location regardless of
source mode.

The storage invariant tests from the companion issue should be in place first: any change to
storage routing that breaks storage uniqueness or semantic equivalence will be caught without
manual inspection of `parsedDoc.rootDoc()`.

## Benefits

- **Synthetic source reconstruction**: the read side has a single authoritative answer for where a
  redirected value lives, rather than reconciling multiple columns.
- **Block loaders**: a central routing owner provides the natural place to define the read
  contract, so block loaders do not re-derive source-mode branching independently.
- **New failure reasons**: adding a constraint type is one additional case in one place rather than
  a new instance of scattered branching.
- **New source modes**: branching logic changes in one place rather than across every call site.

title: [ES|QL] Provide a stable, standalone validation API (Analyzer + Verifier as a JAR) for offline query validation
author: w0rk3r
body:
### Description

Elastic's Threat Research and Detection Engineering team (TRaDE) manages nearly 2,000 prebuilt detection rules. As we migrate rules to ES|QL, we need a programmatic capability to validate each query offline — for syntax, semantics, and schema — across multiple stack versions, without requiring a running Elastic Stack.

Today, ES|QL rules can only be validated remotely against a live cluster. We currently do this via remote validation (https://github.com/elastic/detection-rules/pull/5222), which requires spinning up Elastic containers in CI, only validates against the latest stack version, and adds significant build time and infrastructure cost.

We have prototyped a Java wrapper around the ES|QL validation code in the Elasticsearch repo (POC: https://github.com/elastic/detection-rules/pull/6207). It works well:

- Validating all ES|QL rules went from ~10min (remote) to ~4min, fully local
- We can validate against specific stack versions locally, closing a major gap (today we only validate against latest, so we don't catch ES|QL function backporting issues)
- It retains our required-fields population functionality

However, the POC depends directly on internal Elasticsearch Java interfaces (Analyzer, Verifier, function registry internals), which are not a stable contract and can change between versions — so it isn't a safe long-term integration point.

Following discussion with the ES|QL team (internal: https://github.com/elastic/ia-trade-team/issues/840), the proposed direction is a **well-defined, lean API — effectively the Analyzer + Verifier packaged as a standalone JAR** — that:

- Takes as input: index mappings, and an ES|QL query (including per-query SET settings)
- Returns: success, or VerificationException(s) pointing to the respective parts of the query that are invalid
- Is reflected in Elasticsearch's code as a supported API surface, so consumers aren't broken from one ES version to the next

This issue is to formalize that request and capture the full set of requirements.

## Requirements

Beyond the core validate(mappings, query) → ok | VerificationExceptions contract, our detection rules tooling needs:

- **Referenced-field extraction**: a way to enumerate the fields a query references and outputs. With EQL/KQL we extract fields from the parser AST; for ES|QL we currently return no AST and fall back to fragile regex patterns. This drives Kibana rule features such as required_fields and related_integrations, and validation of rule-type-specific fields (e.g., that alert_suppression.group_by fields exist in the query output). Note: the Analyzer + Verifier does not currently return a list of required fields.
- **Schema validation**: validate field keys and types against different schemas (ECS, integration schemas, beats), supplied as mappings.
- **Version-aware validation**: validate a query against a specific stack version (e.g., 8.19, 9.x), so a single toolchain can validate rules for all supported versions.
- **Semantic/lint-style diagnostics** (nice to have): warnings for ES|QL best practices we currently enforce with regex (e.g., required metadata fields, presence of keep commands) and expensive query patterns.
- **Published, versioned artifact**: released alongside Elasticsearch versions so grammar/verifier parity is maintained as ES|QL evolves.
- **Stability guarantee**: an agreed API surface that Elasticsearch commits to not breaking silently between versions.

## Impact

- Customers may inadvertently receive faulty rules that downstream impact their experience, workloads, or ability to detect & prevent threats
- ES|QL rules cannot be validated offline during development, requiring a running Elastic Stack instance
- CI/CD pipelines require spinning up entire Elastic containers to validate ES|QL queries, adding significant build time and infrastructure costs
- Field extraction from ES|QL queries (required_fields, related_integrations) relies on fragile regex patterns rather than proper AST traversal
- Cannot validate ES|QL queries against specific stack versions without running that version
- Rule authors receive validation feedback late in the development cycle (at CI time) rather than locally during authoring
- Index templates for integrations can have nuances that are only realized upon installing a given integration. Catching these nuances manually and adding fields to a static schema (non-ecs) creates significant manual overhead for our rule developers.

## Use Cases / Examples

- **AST-based field extraction**: With EQL/KQL, we use the parser's AST to extract all referenced fields via `[str(f) for f in self.ast if isinstance(f, eql.ast.Field)]`. For ES|QL, we currently return None from the ast property with a comment: "*Return the AST of the ESQL query. Dependant on an ESQL parser, which is not implemented*"
- **Integration detection**: For EQL/KQL, we parse event.dataset and data_stream.dataset values from the AST. For ES|QL, we use fragile regex patterns that can miss edge cases (e.g., `get_esql_query_event_dataset_integrations()` uses regex like `r"event\.dataset\s+in\s*\(\s*([^)]+)\s*\)"`)
- **Semantic validation**: Currently we validate ES|QL using regex patterns to check for required metadata fields and keep commands - patterns that can easily miss valid ES|QL syntax variations. A concrete example where the regex approach broke down and a proper parser would have made the fix much simpler to build and maintain: https://github.com/elastic/detection-rules/issues/4575
- **Schema validation**: EQL and KQL validators can validate queries against ECS, beats, and integration schemas offline.
- **Alert suppression field validation**: Cannot validate that fields referenced in alert_suppression.group_by exist in the query output without parsing the query
- **Early error detection**: with offline validation in place, issues like https://github.com/elastic/detection-rules/issues/6177 would have been caught early in development instead of after release.
- A large enterprise customer has told us they resorted to reimplementing their own ES|QL parser from the grammar to overcome the same gaps.

## Stopgaps

- **Remote validation** (https://github.com/elastic/detection-rules/pull/5222): requires managing a combination of infrastructure — N stack releases, N integrations per rule, N rule variations and indices — causing significant overhead and complexity, leading to many issues that require investigation and manual work to resolve

## Asks / Recommendations

- Define and expose the Analyzer + Verifier as a standalone, supported API/JAR with the contract outlined above (mappings + query in; ok/VerificationExceptions out)
- Include or complement it with referenced-field/AST access so downstream tooling can extract fields without regex
- Support validation against specific stack versions, and publish the artifact per release so grammar parity is maintained
- Document the supported API surface and its stability guarantees

## References

- Internal (Elastic): https://github.com/elastic/security-team/issues/11554 (Epic: ES|QL python parser — original requirements), https://github.com/elastic/ia-trade-team/issues/840 (Meta: Java ES|QL validation — POC and API discussion with the ES|QL team)
- POC: https://github.com/elastic/detection-rules/pull/6207
- Remote validation: https://github.com/elastic/detection-rules/pull/5222
- Examples the parser would improve/catch: https://github.com/elastic/detection-rules/issues/4575, https://github.com/elastic/detection-rules/issues/6177
- EQL parser we rely on for EQL today: https://github.com/endgameinc/eql
